headline: Smart balls, sensor-laden shirts, and the quest for granular sports data
summary: The founders of Sportable are using sensors to provide a wealth of information for athletes, teams and fans
body_text:
Sportable co-founders Dugald Macdonald and Peter Husemeyer believe sport remains one of the last major industries to be fully digitised. Their London-based company embeds sensors into balls and player equipment to produce real-time data used by teams, leagues and broadcasters, across a host of sports including American football, rugby, football and tennis.

Speaking with the FT’s sports business correspondent, Samuel Agini, in London, they discuss closing “dark patches” of data, the rise of personalised broadcasts and how smart equipment could reshape the business of sport.

Samuel Agini: What is Sportable? Can we have a short history and what the business looks like today?

Dugald Macdonald: At a high level, Sportable is a data, insight and content platform. We serve data- and insight-hungry stakeholders across the sports ecosystem: teams for performance analysis, leagues for game management and officiating, and the commercial ecosystem including video games, fantasy sports groups, be
